title: Add basic locking to Libartifact
body:
Lock access to and modification of the index.json file, to ensure concurrent addition/removal does not result in lost state. Use a standard c/storage lockfile, making use of its r/w locking ability to support concurrent access, only serializing writes.

This is not a very efficient locking scheme around artifact removal and - especially - addition. I view this as the first step, establishing any sort of mutual exclusion to prevent state corruption. Step 2 is to adapt the staged removal work being done to make image removal require only minimal use of locks, ensuring it works with artifact addition. This staged addition means we won't have to hold the lock for the full artifact pull.

There's a minor problem here that it is not obvious what c/image endpoints read from/write to index.json. I think I've caught everything but I could be incorrect. A future implementation could provide callback functions to c/image that would allow locking access, but the design for that is potentially quite complex. Documentation on the OCI layout functions as to whether they read or write could be a good alternative?

comments.body: The read-only locks are *probably* safe to drop, which will make things better than they are now, but I wanted to keep them in for discussion (and because it's definitely safer to have them).
[NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.
Relaxed the locking around Add() a bit at suggestion of @baude - I'll check if more is possible on Monday.
LGTM, I suppose any tests would be racey ?
Yes, there isn't an easy way to test this.

Can you squash the commits? Fixes for previous commits in the same PR should just be squashed.

As for testing it would make no sense to test this in our regular test suites. But long term I think we need some kind of test suite that can run a ton of things in parallel and then ensure the states are consistent, maybe a bit like a fuzzer. artifacts are just one small thing I am more concerned about containers and normal storage state.

> But long term I think we need some kind of test suite that can run a ton of things in parallel and then ensure the states are consistent, maybe a bit like a fuzzer. artifacts are just one small thing I am more concerned about containers and normal storage state.

Eventually, https://pkg.go.dev/testing/synctest@master will land. The idea of a “bubble” where sleeps can be used to ensure deterministic ordering _and_ where they don’t actually spend time waiting is intriguing; it seems to allow writing deterministic locking tests and reason about code coverage.
